mechanism: Garlic’s effects are proposed to relate to reactive sulfur compounds formed from alliin (including allicin) that may influence vascular tone, platelet activity, and lipid metabolism. These mechanisms are biologically plausible but do not by themselves establish clinical efficacy for any disease outcome.
safetyNotes: Oral garlic is generally well tolerated in studies, with common adverse effects including odor, GI discomfort, and nausea. Topical raw garlic can cause severe skin irritation or burns. Garlic supplements can increase bleeding risk, especially around procedures or when combined with anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy.
description: Garlic (Allium sativum) is widely used as both a food and an herbal supplement. Clinical evidence suggests small average benefits for LDL/total cholesterol, blood pressure, and blood glucose in some populations, but effects are modest and not a substitute for standard care.
dosage: No single standardized dose is established across products. Use product-labeled dosing and clinical guidance, especially when bleeding risk or polypharmacy is present.
interactions: Potential additive bleeding risk with anticoagulants, antiplatelet drugs, and aspirin.
Discuss supplement use before surgery or invasive procedures due to bleeding risk.
legalStatus: Widely approved as food and supplement globally (GRAS).
preparation: Common preparations include fresh cloves in food, dehydrated garlic powders, aged garlic extracts, and oils. Raw topical application is not considered safe due to risk of skin injury.
region: Cultivated worldwide; historically used across multiple medical traditions including Mediterranean and Asian systems.
sideEffects: may cause bleeding in high doses (anticoagulant effect)
